---
title: "Mapping with ggplot2"
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Seeding

## Libraries

```{r setup}
library(tidyverse)          # ggplot2, dplyr, readr, etc.
library(maps)               # basic country maps
library(patchwork)          # combining multiple plots
library(marmap)             # bathymetry data
```

## Data used

```{r}
minke   <- read_csv("data/minke.csv")
iceland <- map_data(map = "world", region = "Iceland")
smb     <- read_csv("data/smb_summary.csv")
xlim <- c(-30.0, -10.0)
ylim <- c( 62.5,  67.5)
depth <- 
  getNOAA.bathy(lon1 = xlim[1], lon2 = xlim[2],
                lat1 = ylim[1], lat2 = ylim[2],
                resolution = 1) |> 
  fortify() |>     # turn the object into a data.frame
  filter(z <= 0)   # limit to our area of interest
```

# Basis of ggplot

## The 3 key compontents

1. A **data** argument — a `data.frame` or derivative (`tibble`, `sf`, ...)
2. A set of **aesthetic mappings** via `aes()` — connecting variables to visual properties
3. At least one **layer** via a `geom_*()` function — describing how to render observations

```{r}
ggplot(data = minke) +
  aes(x = lon, y = lat, colour = sex) +
  geom_point()
```

Different syntax, equivalent outcome — all four lines below produce the same plot:

```{r eval=FALSE}
ggplot(data = minke)                       + geom_point(aes(x = lon, y = lat, colour = sex))
ggplot(minke)                              + geom_point(aes(lon,         lat, colour = sex))
ggplot()                                   + geom_point(data = minke, aes(lon, lat, colour = sex))
ggplot(minke, aes(lon, lat, colour = sex)) + geom_point()
```

## You can save a plot (object or image)

A ggplot can be stored as an object for later use and built upon:

```{r}
#| eval: false
p <- ggplot(minke) + geom_point(aes(lon, lat, colour = sex))
class(p)                     # "ggplot2::ggplot" "ggplot" "ggplot2::gg" "S7_object" "gg"
p |> write_rds("minke.rds")  # you can save it
read_rds("minke.rds")        # and retrive it (later or send it to somebody else)
```

Save as image:

```{r}
#| eval: false
p |> ggsave(filename = "minke.png") # check the help file for all the options
```

Include in a(nother) document:

```{r}
#| eval: false
knitr::include_graphics("minke.png")
```

# Quick and dirty backgrounds

Let's take a peek at the object "iceland" already imported above:

```{r}
#| eval: false
iceland |> glimpse()    # coordinates are "long" and "lat"
                        # "order" matters
```

Plotting with different geoms illustrates why order matter:

```{r fig.height=6}
p <- ggplot(iceland, aes(long, lat))
p1 <- p + geom_point()   + labs(title = "geom_point")
p2 <- p + geom_line()    + labs(title = "geom_line")
p3 <- p + geom_path()    + labs(title = "geom_path")
p4 <- p + geom_polygon() + labs(title = "geom_polygon")
p1 + p2 + p3 + p4  # The magic of the patchwork-package
```

* `geom_line` does not work because it reorders the data
  * Check help file for `geom_line` vs `geom_path`
* `geom_point` reveals the data "richness" - dataset has only 454 rows
  * This is an endless issue!

# Projections

A map is just a xy-plot but with a certain projections. We could try to guess the projections (i.e. the aspect ration of the plot) as done on the left, or better still use the `geom_quickmap` function (on the right):

TODO: Need some fix, this is not illustrating the issue

```{r, fig.height = 2, fig.width = 2}
p4 <- p + geom_path() +                            labs(title = "as is")
p5 <- p + geom_path() + coord_fixed(ratio = 2.4) + labs(title = "coord_fixed")
p6 <- p + geom_path() + coord_quickmap()         + labs(title = "coord_quickmap")
p4 + p5 + p6 + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 2, 4))
```

* A spatial map in its simplest term is just an xy-plot with a specific aspect ratio
* You don't want to use `coord_fixed` because the aspect ratio depends on latitude 
* `coord_quickmap` is only an approximation (`coord_map` may be more accurate)
  * Actually all maps are wrong when put on a two-dimensional pane.

# Layers

## Multiple layers

Let's create our "perfect" background map:

```{r}
#| echo: false
basemap <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = iceland, 
               aes(x = long, y = lat),
               fill = "grey80", colour = "white") +
  coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  theme_bw()
```

We can now use our base map for continuous analysis.

## A pitfall to avoid

Let's say you want to zoom into area (like here, NorthEast)

NOTE: Not the best of examples:

```{r}
dont <- basemap + xlim(-30, -12) + ylim(65, 67)
do   <- basemap + coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-30, -12), ylim = c(65, 67))
dont + do
```

## geom_point — bubble plots

Mapping a continuous variable to `size` creates a bubble chart. Use `scale_size_area()` so that **area** (not radius) is proportional to the value, and zero value maps to zero area:

```{r}
minke <- basemap +
  geom_point(data = minke,
             aes(lon, lat, size = age),           # so not long-lat
             alpha = 0.3, colour = "red") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 10)
cod <- basemap +
  geom_point(data = smb |> filter(year == 2019),  # you can filter in-place
             aes(lon1, lat1, size = cod_n),       # here lon1 and lat1
             alpha = 0.3, colour = "red") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 10)
minke + cod
```

## geom_segment — tow tracks

`geom_segment()` draws straight lines between start and end coordinates, ideal for a quickmap of e.g. tow tracks:

```{r}
basemap +
  geom_segment(data = smb |> filter(year == 2019),
               aes(x = lon1, y = lat1, xend = lon2, yend = lat2),
               linewidth = 0.3)
```

## geom_raster — gridded summaries

`geom_raster()` draws rectangles centered on each (x, y) coordinate, useful for pre-gridded data:

```{r}
s <- smb |> filter(year == 2019) |> 
  group_by(ir_lon, ir_lat) |>                    # lon-lat: 1.0 x 0.5 degrees 
  summarise(Cod = mean(cod_n), .groups = "drop")
basemap + 
  geom_raster(data = s,
              aes(ir_lon, ir_lat, fill = Cod)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1, name = "Mean cod (n)")
```


## geom_path — vessel tracks

`geom_path()` connects points **in the order they appear in the data**, making it ideal for time-ordered GPS tracks:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#track <- read_csv("data/is_smb_vms2019.csv")
#track <- read_csv("data/small_vms.csv")
basemap +
  geom_path(data = track,
            aes(x = lon, y = lat, colour = factor(id)),
            linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(name = "Vessel", palette = "Set1")
```

## A little bit on colour scales

### Continuous data: viridis palettes

The **viridis** palettes (`"viridis"`, `"magma"`, `"plasma"`, `"inferno"`, `"cividis"`, `"mako"`, `"rocket"`, `"turbo"`) are built into ggplot2. They are perceptually uniform, readable in greyscale, and accessible to colour-blind readers:

```{r}
p <- ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = s, 
            aes(x = ir_lon, y = ir_lat, fill = Cod)) +
  geom_polygon(data = iceland,
               aes(long, lat),
               fill = "grey60") +
  coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  theme_bw()

p1 <- p + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D", name = "viridis")
p2 <- p + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1, name = "magma (rev)")
p3 <- p + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C", name = "plasma")

p1 + p2 + p3
```

### Colour scale transformations

When data are highly skewed, transforming the colour scale can reveal spatial patterns more clearly. The `transform` argument accepts standard transformation names:

```{r}
p_log  <- p + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1,
                                         transform = "log1p",
                                         name = "log1p scale")
p_sqrt <- p + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1,
                                         transform = "sqrt",
                                         name = "sqrt scale")
p_log + p_sqrt
```

### Discrete intervals with `cut()`

Binning continuous data into labelled classes can be clearer than a smooth gradient, especially for reporting:

```{r}
s_cut <- s %>%
  mutate(Cod_class = cut(Cod,
                         breaks = c(0, 23, 62, 145, Inf),
                         labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very high"),
                         include.lowest = TRUE))
ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = s_cut, aes(x = ir_lon, y = ir_lat, fill = Cod_class)) +
  geom_polygon(data = iceland,
               aes(long, lat),
               fill = "grey60") +
  coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "B", direction = -1,
                       name = "Cod abundance",
                       na.value = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  theme_bw()
```

## geom_contour

For the example we use a high resolution gridded data

```{r}
p <-
  ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = depth, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = z)) +
  geom_polygon(data = iceland,
               aes(long, lat),
               fill = "grey60") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D", name = "Depth (m)") +
  coord_quickmap() +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  theme_bw()
p
```

Add depth contour lines with `geom_contour()`:

```{r}
p +
  geom_contour(data = depth,
               aes(x = x, y = y, z = z),
               breaks = c(-50, -100, -200, -400, -1000),
               linewidth = 0.3, colour = "white")
```

# Readings:

The [ggplot2 book](https://ggplot2-book.org) is the definitive reference for everything covered here and much more.
